title: The Unheard Outcry of Manipur: An Essay on Hoihnu Hauzel's 'Stories The Fire Could Not Burn'
summary:
Stories the Fire Could Not Burn by Hoihnu Hauzel (Speaking Tiger Books) is a narrative non‑fiction account of the Manipur crisis (2023–25) told from within the violence. Hauzel centers personal testimonies—her family, displaced Kuki‑Zomi/Mizo people and eyewitnesses—to show how everyday life, memory and belonging were shattered, arguing that long‑standing state policies, communal narratives and political indifference shaped and amplified the violence rather than resolving underlying tensions.

The book documents specific events and structural processes that preceded and accompanied the unrest: administrative and ideological moves (including the long enforcement of AFSPA and decisions framed as development or forest protection), targeted demolitions and evictions, episodes of coordinated attack and apparent police inaction, and the dual role of social media in both exposing atrocities and retraumatizing survivors. Hauzel records incidents such as the 14 April 2023 approval of 188 religious structures (Hindu temples) and subsequent church demolitions, the forcible eviction of K. Songjang village on 20 February 2023, the outbreak of large‑scale violence from 3 May 2023 after an ATSUM solidarity march related to Meitei demands for Scheduled Tribe status, and the human consequences—loss of life, mass displacement and the erosion of ‘home’—while framing the book as an archive resisting erasure.

Key new and salient facts and details: author and title (Hoihnu Hauzel, Stories the Fire Could Not Burn); timeline markers (K. Songjang eviction 20 Feb 2023; government decision on 14 Apr 2023; violence outbreak 3 May 2023); reported human toll within six months (nearly 200 dead, over 70,000 displaced); named incidents and witnesses (Letminthang’s brutal killing and public burning; attacks on Paite Veng and churches declared ‘unauthorized’; destruction of Hauzel’s family brickfield); administrative measures cited (re‑classification of hill settlements as reserved/protected forest, 2015 identification of 48 villages as eco‑sensitive); political context (state leaders Nongthombam Biren Singh and Yumnam Khemchand Singh from BJP and Meitei community) and displacement destinations (Assam, Mizoram); and the book’s insistence that personal testimony functions as a vital archive against official silence, selective narratives and possible erasure.
importance_reasoning: The item documents large‑scale communal violence with a reported human toll of nearly 200 deaths and over 70,000 displaced within six months. It is not merely an opinion piece but a narrative non‑fiction account of ongoing, deadly unrest. It meets the stated threshold for existential importance (involves more than a hundred deaths) and therefore qualifies as an event of global/existential importance under the API rules.
article_text:
Stories the Fire Could Not Burn by Hoihnu Hauzel; Published by Speaking Tiger Books
A state is not defined by those who govern it or by its formal ties with the central authority. It is defined by its people, their lived realities, the conditions they endure, and the circumstances that shape their everyday lives. In this sense, Jammu & Kashmir is often described as a ‘critical state’, not merely for its geopolitical position but for the way its functioning is deeply controlled by the centre, largely through military presence, with little space for meaningful dialogue.
Such control has done little to bridge the distance between Kashmiris and the rest of the country. Instead, a dominant narrative has taken root, one that casts Jammu & Kashmir as a breeding ground of terrorism and its people as disloyal, even celebratory of India’s defeats. This perception has been strengthened over time by external conflicts, particularly Pakistan’s interventions, as well as by India’s own inability to address the region’s concerns through sustained political engagement.
Key moments, such as the unresolved question of the plebiscite and the abrogation of Article 370 without consulting the people of the state, have deepened this sense of alienation. In many ways, this reflects a recurring history, when aspirations for autonomy or independence of the people of Jammu & Kashmir during and after the partition were overshadowed by the competing interests of two nations, each seeking to contain the region within its own political framework.
When it comes to Manipur, a similar condition has been allowed to grow, shaped by successive governments, and pushed to an extreme under the present regime. The difference, however, lies in visibility. Unlike Kashmir, a North-Eastern state like Manipur remains largely absent from the concerns of mainstream India. Its suffering unfolds quietly, receiving little sustained attention. This neglect is evident in the way the ongoing violence, often described as an ethnic conflict between the Kuki-Zomi/Mizo communities and the Meiteis, has been treated.
Since its outbreak on 3rd May 2023, and even as it continues, the crisis of Manipur has struggled to find consistent space in mainstream media narratives. The absence is not merely journalistic. It is also political. Even the Prime Minister Narendra Modi, has not visited the state during this prolonged period of unrest. This silence becomes more striking given that the state leadership, including Nongthombam Biren Singh and Yumnam Khemchand Singh, belong to the same ruling party, the Bharatiya Janata Party, and is drawn from the Meitei community, one of the principal groups involved in the conflict. In such a situation, the absence of dialogue and attention only deepens the sense of distance, leaving the crisis to persist in the margins of national consciousness.
Hoihnu Hauzel’s Stories the Fire Could Not Burn: A Personal Account of the Manipur Crisis: 2023-25, does not attempt to position itself within the familiar frameworks of political analysis or journalistic reporting of the Manipur conflict. It refuses the distance that such modes or professions often demand. Instead, this work of narrative non-fiction speaks from within the wound. What we encounter here is not documentation of the state politics, but a lived testimony and an urgent, unfiltered outcry chiselled by Hauzel’s own proximity to violence and loss.
Hauzel writes as someone who has witnessed, in immediate and unsettling ways, how linguistic, ethnic and communal identity can become the basis for brutality. Her narratives are not structured around statistics or official accounts, but around people or individuals whose lives have been irreversibly altered by the state. In choosing to orient these personal stories towards the readers, she draws attention to the fragility of human existence within conflict, where the loss of a home is inseparable from the loss of memory, belonging, and safety.
There is also a conscious insistence in her work on recording what might otherwise be dismissed. Individual suffering, she suggests, is often rendered invisible unless it is expressed with a force that demands acknowledgement. The ache that runs through her writing becomes a form of resistance, something that cannot be substituted by policy language or absorbed into diplomatic rhetoric. It is precisely this emotional intensity that gives her account its ethical weight.
At the same time, the book does not shy away from confronting the asymmetries within the conflict. It is navigated towards the unequal distribution of power, suggesting that certain communities bear a disproportionate burden of violence and displacement. In doing so, Hauzel underscores a difficult but necessary truth which is, that in any conflict, naming the structures and agents of oppression is essential, even when such naming unsettles dominant narratives or political constructs and decorum of the nation.
Her voice remains firmly personal, grounded in experience rather than mediated interpretation. This makes her account resistant to appropriation. It neither can be polished into neutrality, nor redefined to fit the demands of national image makers. Any attempt to do so would risk erasing the very pain it seeks to convey. In this sense, misdirecting readers by dilution of the conflict’s objective and narrative in the name of balance, decorum, or political convenience, especially at a time when such notions themselves appear increasingly unstable, will be unjust to those who carry the pain of losing the people they loved, resources they lived on and places they inhabited.
In the preface of this book, Hauzel confronts, with clarity and defiance, the charge of bias. She acknowledges that some may call her account biased and leaves room for that, for what is often dismissed as bias is, in truth, an urgent need to speak for those whose suffering remains unseen within dominant narratives. In this assertion, she reframes bias not as distortion, but as a necessary act of witnessing.
This need arises from the larger indifference that shapes how much of India perceives Manipur and, more broadly, the North-East. Prejudice often enters quietly but firmly, through the casual racism that reduces people to their physical features, making them feel like outsiders. Through the policing of food habits, where what one eats becomes a marker of impurity, the rest of the India excludes them on a personal level. And through a deeper ignorance that treats the region as distant and peripheral because the North-East remains tenuously connected to the rest of the country through the narrow stretch known as the Siliguri Corridor, often called the ‘Chicken’s Neck’, a fact that itself becomes a metaphor for its fragile inclusion within the national imagination.
This exclusion is not only social but also political and historical. The long enforcement of the Armed Forces (Special Powers) Act (AFSPA) in Manipur, which has structurally defined the everyday life under militarization, has rarely received any sustained national concern. Acts of resistance too, have often been overlooked, such as the protest of the Meira Paibis, who, in an extraordinary moment of defiance, stripped themselves to challenge the violence and impunity of military power. Even in the present, reports of humiliation and brutality of men and women publicly stripped, beaten, and killed, struggle to receive the attention they demand.
To deepen this narrative of exclusion, Hauzel recalls an incident that unsettles even her own sense of grief, making her struggle feel smaller in comparison to others. She writes about Letminthang, a Kuki man employed in the Income Tax Department, whose death was marked not only by brutality but by spectacle. He was dragged through the streets, stabbed, beaten with rods, and forced into acts of humiliation against his own community. And when he was already at the edge of death, the mob chose to burn his body by pouring petrol over him, turning this entire act violence into a public statement.
Hauzel reflects that what happened to Letminthang was not merely a killing. It was a message that’s inscribed in violence, intended to erase not just a person, but an identity, a community, a name. In recounting this, her writing moves beyond mourning into a sharper recognition of what such acts signify within a larger pattern of conflict. That conflict, she writes, is neither sudden nor isolated. Its roots stretch back to the years following independence, shaped by years of tensions between communities. Within Manipur, the Meitei community, including Meitei Hindus and Meitei Pangal, has often occupied a position of dominance in the valley, while tribal communities such as the Kuki-Zomi/Mizo and the Naga populations have largely inhabited the hills.
Hauzel points to the way certain narratives have been constructed and circulated, particularly the claim that tribal communities occupy 90% of Manipur. While it is true that a large portion of the state is covered by hills, she notes that this geography is frequently misunderstood or misrepresented. Much of this terrain is not uniformly habitable, and the realities of land, access, and settlement are far more complex than such claims suggest. Yet, both the state and central authorities have often failed to address or clarify these nuances, allowing oversimplified narratives to harden into sources of resentment and division.
There are reasons why such crucial realities remain ignored. Beneath the surface of conflict lies an economic motive, one that’s tethered to land, resources and control. The push for ‘development’, often shaped by capitalist interests and supported by dominant groups within Manipur, increasingly turns toward the exploitation of natural resources, much of which lie in the hilly regions inhabited by the tribal communities.
In this context, divisive narratives do not emerge accidentally. They serve a purpose or an objective. By portraying hill communities, particularly the Kuki-Zomi/Mizo groups, as occupying disproportionate space, a justification is created for intervention, displacement, and eventual control. As these narratives circulate, they enable both the state and the dominant sections of society to legitimise the gradual erosion of minority settlements while larger economic interests move in to claim land and resources. What develops then is an alignment between the capitalists and the ones who dominate the state and this particular alignment is symbiotic by nature.
At the same time, the realities of life in the hills complicate this picture. Limited infrastructure, lack of access to education, healthcare, and economic opportunities compel many from these communities to move toward the plains. The plains, particularly Imphal, become necessary spaces for survival, not sites of encroachment. Yet, this movement is reframed within a different narrative, one that suggests that the Kuki-Zomi/Mizo communities are taking over the valley and displacing the Meiteis from their own land and rights. It is a propagandist tool that is soaked in hatred.
Such a narrative, which is repeated and reinforced constantly, leads to the transformation of anxiety into hostility. It produces a sense of threatened ownership among the majority even when the movement of people is driven by necessity of individual development rather than territorial expansion. But to fulfil the interest of the majority, the violence that erupted in Manipur was a way to reclaim what does not belong to a single community. It was an assertive move to control the state in presence of a political regime which has always supported the Hindutva school of division.
In the opening chapter, That Dark Night, Hauzel brings together the lived experiences of the Kuki-Zomi/Mizo community with a wider political critique. She narrates the suffering of her own family alongside that of other affected households, but does not allow these stories to remain merely personal. Instead, she places them within a larger structure of state action, showing how policies, administrative decisions, and ideological agendas have steadily shaped, and often worsened, the conditions of minority communities in Manipur. Although many of these developments were documented in national level newspapers, Hauzel suggests that the dominant public discourse has consistently flattened or ignored the complex realities of North-East India.
One of the most striking examples she discusses is the decision taken on 14 April 2023, when the Manipur government approved the construction of 188 religious structures across the state. Notably, all of these were Hindu temples. This move appears deeply disproportionate when placed against the demographic composition of the region. As per the 2011 census, a significant portion of Manipur’s population belongs to minority communities, including the Kuki-Zomi/Mizo tribes, many of whom are Christian. The implementation of this decision was not merely symbolic. It took a material and confrontational form, as churches were demolished using JCB bulldozers. These actions were met with strong resistance from Kuki organizations, who viewed them as direct attacks on their faith and identity. But the message was loud and clear. Under the rule of Hindu-extremist government, the majority community of Manipur, who are mostly Meitei Hindus, are going to benefit and faith here will play a big role in fuelling this divisive narrative.
Alongside this, Hauzel traces the emergence of a powerful and troubling narrative, in which the Kuki community is encroaching upon and ‘taking over’ land in Manipur. This narrative, she argues, has been instrumental in legitimizing state-led eviction drives. One such case is the eviction of K. Songjang village. Here, older legal frameworks, particularly those related to forest protection introduced after 1972, were brought back into focus. These laws enabled the state to classify lands inhabited by Kuki-Zomi/Mizo communities as reserved or protected forests, thereby rendering long-standing settlements as illegal ones in administrative terms.
The situation became even more precarious in 2015, when the Union Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change identified forty-eight additional villages as eco-sensitive zones. The implication was that these areas could be transformed into wildlife sanctuaries, further displacing indigenous populations. For the Kuki community, such measures were not just environmental policies but existential threats, as they risked the loss of ancestral land, traditional rights, and homes. But it was a calculated move by the people in power, who are Meiteis, to take control of not only Manipur but also those who live in the circle of being in minority.
Unsurprisingly, these developments sparked protests. Kuki chieftains and community leaders openly opposed what they saw as unjust and unlawful policies. They argued that such decisions were made without proper consultation and disproportionately targeted tribal populations. Hauzel underscores that the tension reached a critical point on 20 February 2023 after the eviction of the K. Songjang village. On that day, state authorities arrived with six JCB bulldozers and forcefully evicted sixteen Kuki-Zomi/Mizo households. The operation, carried out under the justification of clearing encroachment on protected forest land, was marked by aggression and violence. It left behind not only destroyed homes but also a deep sense of insecurity and dispossession. The protests still remain unheard and, for the mainstream India, the rights of the majority became primary than the insecurity of the Kuki-Zomi/Mizo community.
The violence and ethnic tensions in Manipur, Hauzel reminds us, are not sudden or without history. What unfolded in recent years is rooted in older cracks, long defined by questions of identity, land and political recognition. Yet, the immediate trigger for the prolonged civil unrest, she notes, was the solidarity march organized by the All-Tribal Students’ Union of Manipur (ATSUM). The march was a response to the demand by the Meitei community for Scheduled Tribe (ST) status, a classification originally meant to safeguard historically marginalized tribal groups who lack equal access to resources and opportunities. Hauzel presents this demand as deeply contentious, especially when extended to a community perceived to already possess relative social, economic, and political advantages.
The protest, held in Lamka town of Churachandpur, began as a peaceful assertion of dissent. But, as Hauzel carefully traces, that fragile peace soon gave way to spiralling violence. Within six months of the crisis, nearly 200 lives were lost, and over 70,000 people, predominantly from the Kuki-Zomi/Mizo communities were displaced from their homes. What began as a political disagreement soon turned into a humanitarian catastrophe, reshaping the landscape of everyday life in the region.
Hauzel strengthens her account by recording the testimony of witnesses, one of whom recalls that it felt as though the Meiteis had come prepared. This sense of premeditation can be found throughout her narrative. From the targeting of symbolic structures such as the Kuki Anglo War Memorial Gate to the systematic destruction of churches belonging to the Kuki-Zomi/Mizo community, the violence appeared neither random nor spontaneous. Instead, it was planned and coordinated. Equally disturbing, she observes, was the apparent inaction or absolute surrender of police forces and those in positions of authority, who failed to intervene meaningfully as the attacks unfolded.
The violence, however, is not only described at the level of collective trauma. Hauzel also brings it painfully close to her own family and self. She recounts how one of the earliest attacks by the Meitei mob was directed at her family’s brickfield. This was not just a site of economic activity but a space tied to memory of a Manipur which she grew up in. The brickfield provided employment to many, and its destruction affected not only her family but also those who depended on it. In Hauzel’s telling, this moment becomes deeply personal when the loss of property merged with the loss of a sense of belonging, and the rupture of a past that once felt stable and peaceful.
Hauzel’s narrative does not reduce the conflict to a uniform story of violence. She carefully acknowledges that, even in the midst of chaos, there were Meitei families who helped those fleeing from the mobs, offering shelter and guidance to them in whatever way possible. Some officials, too, acted with urgency and conscience, choosing to move vulnerable families to safer places without waiting for formal authorization. These moments of humanity cannot overshadow the larger narrative of brutality, reminding the reader that compassion did persist, albeit for a brief time.
Yet, Hauzel is equally clear that such acts, however meaningful, could not outweigh the scale and intensity of the violence that unfolded. A few individuals could not undo what a larger, organized force inflicted upon communities that were already vulnerable which held people with limited resources, no alternative homeland, and little institutional protection even when they participated in the electoral system of the state of Manipur. The imbalance of power becomes starkly visible in her account, as those who were attacked had neither the means to resist nor the assurance of safety.
She turns to the example of Paite Veng, a prominent settlement of the Paite community, to illustrate how targeted and systematic the violence appeared. The attack on this locality did not emerge in isolation. Rather, it was preceded by a government order issued in April 2023. According to Hauzel, three churches, each with histories spanning over fifty years, were suddenly declared ‘unauthorized’ and marked for demolition on the grounds that they stood on government land. This decision was abrupt and unsettling and it heightened tensions within the minority community and created a precarious atmosphere that seemed to anticipate further conflict.
What followed, Hauzel suggests, bore the marks of coordinated attack rather than spontaneity. The assault on Paite Veng and its churches appeared organized, with a striking level of precision in its execution. Notably, while homes belonging to the Kuki-Zomi/Mizo community were attacked and set ablaze, Meitei households in the same area were left untouched. To Hauzel, this selective destruction indicates that the attackers were not acting blindly. They seemed to know exactly which properties to target. The pattern of violence thus reinforces her broader argument that these events were not random outbreaks of anger but carried elements of planning and intent.
Hauzel, while writing about the incident in Paite Veng, raises many difficult and unsettling questions about the role of state authority. The sequence of administrative decisions followed by targeted violence creates, in her view, was an impression of complicity or, at the very least, a failure of accountability. Through this, she underscores that when power, policy, and prejudice intersect, the consequences are borne most heavily by those already living on the margins.
It is important for readers to move beyond the political framework of the conflict and confront its deeply human cost. Hauzel urges us to recognize what violence meant at a personal level, how people were forced to witness the killing of their loved ones, the destruction of their homes, and the violation of their dignity in public. These were not distant events but intimate ruptures, where memory, identity, and belonging were violently reshaped for their own future. The past itself was targeted, as if erasing it could compel people to accept an imposed and newly constructed present.
Through her documentation, especially the story of her niece, alongside testimonies from other eyewitnesses, Hauzel reveals a version of Manipur that remains largely absent from official narratives. It is a Manipur that many will not encounter in textbooks or state-sanctioned historical documents in the future. She suggests that this absence is not accidental, but part of a broader tendency of those in power to suppress uncomfortable truths to shield both the present and future generations from confronting state failures.
In this sense, the book becomes more than a personal account and transforms into a vital archive. What begins as individual testimonies acquires collective significance, preserving fragments of truth that might otherwise be erased. Hauzel’s work stands as a reminder that when institutions fail to record honestly, personal narratives often become the only means through which reality survives. Her reflections also point to a larger pattern in the writing of history itself. History, she suggests, is often defined and distorted by those who hold power. Whether one looks at events like the American Civil War, the Spanish Civil War, or even more recent episodes of unrest such as the 2020 Delhi riots, the narratives that reach the public are frequently contested, selective, and at times deliberately altered. What is presented as ‘history’ may obscure as much as it reveals.
For Hauzel, then, the responsibility shifts to push the readers to question, to investigate, and to listen closely to voices that emerge from the margins. It is often through these voices that one can begin to understand what truly happened, who suffered, who wielded power, and how violence was justified or concealed by the dominant groups. In preserving these testimonies, her work resists erasure and insists on a more honest, if uncomfortable, engagement with the horrific past, which is still the present since Manipur is still facing unrests in several places.
Displacement, Hauzel suggests, may seem like an escape from immediate violence, but it carries a quiet and enduring cost. To leave is not simply to move from one place to another. It is to abandon everything that defines one’s identity, home, memory, community, and belonging. For the Kuki people who managed to flee in order to survive, survival itself came at the price of rupture of their own self. They were forced to leave behind not only their houses and land, but also the emotional and cultural worlds that defined them as individuals and as part of a larger community.
Many sought refuge in neighbouring states such as Assam and Mizoram. These places offered shelter and relative safety yet they remained in unfamiliar terrains. The displaced lived in a state of convolution where they were physically removed from violence, yet emotionally tethered to it. News of continuing unrest kept reaching them, keeping their fear alive and preventing any sense of stability, even with a sense of protection. In this suspended existence, they waited for intervention, for justice, for a return that seemed increasingly uncertain.
Hauzel portrays this waiting as deeply painful, especially in the face of what she sees as prolonged inaction. For over two years, the crisis persisted without meaningful resolution which left the state effectively isolated. She suggests that this absence of decisive intervention was not merely a failure, but something more troubling. It was an indifference, or even a political calculation, where unrest served certain interests by diverting attention and enabling other forms of control.
At the same time, she notes that the violence did not go entirely unchallenged. Opposition groups and voices beyond the region condemned what was happening, drawing attention to the scale of suffering. Yet, in her account, such criticism did not translate into protection of those who were exiled from their very state. Instead, the situation was navigated towards a larger ideological project, arguing that the marginalization of the Kuki community is tied to an attempt to redefine the social and political fabric of Manipur in a particular direction. With a Hindu-dominated Meitei population, the dream of having a Hindu Rashtra will strengthen for the ruling government.
As global conflicts unfold, such as the ongoing tensions involving Iran, Israel, and the United States, we are reminded of how deeply violence is mediated through social media. Hauzel reflects on this unsettling reality that social media often turns suffering into a vicious spectacle, drawing viewers through graphic imagery that provokes empathy, outrage, and, at times, even financial engagement. In this digital economy of attention, pain becomes visible only when it is dramatized.
Yet, she also acknowledges a paradox. The same social media that commodifies violence can also disrupt silence. In the case of Manipur, it played a crucial role in forcing the mainstream India to confront a crisis it had long ignored. Images, testimonies, and fragments of suffering broke through the indifference to familiarize the rest of the country with what had been systematically overlooked, particularly the suffering of the Kuki community in the hands of the Meiteis.
In the chapter War of Words, Hauzel examines this tension closely. She points out that while graphic representations of violence helped draw national attention, they also carried a heavy emotional burden for those who had already endured displacement and trauma. For survivors, these circulating images were not distant realities but painful reminders of what they had lost and escaped. At the same time, she turns a critical eye toward mainstream media. According to her, many media houses hesitated to fully report on the violence in Manipur. She suggests several reasons for this silence, first being the political context of a state governed by the Bharatiya Janata Party, second being the perceived lack of commercial incentive in challenging dominant narratives, and finally, the risks faced by journalists and sympathizers who attempted to present an honest account. In such an environment, truth itself becomes precarious. For this very reason, Hauzel puts a very important question before the readers, what is the purpose of life, if being authentically oneself can summon such hatred?
Stories the Fire Could Not Burn: A Personal Account of the Manipur Crisis: 2023-25 ultimately moves beyond viewing the events in Manipur as just another episode of political unrest. Instead, Hauzel frames it as something more fundamental and troubling. She suggests that the violence was not merely about power struggles, but about systematically redefining the social fabric of the state by targeting the minority in ways that resemble ethnic cleansing rather than any conventional conflict. At its core, however, the book remains deeply personal. It is anchored in love for a homeland that is rapidly transforming in the presence of hatred and hegemony. Through Hauzel’s eyes, we witness not only the physical destruction of places and communities but also the erosion of meaning attached to ‘home’ itself. This book offers us an unrecognizable Manipur that changed within a short span of time leaving behind nothing but loss of memory and an uncertain future.

Get the latest CounterCurrents updates delivered straight to your inbox.
Kabir Deb is a writer, book and film reviewer based in Karimganj, Assam. He is the recipient of Social Journalism Award, 2017; Reuel International Award for Best Upcoming poet, 2019; Nissim International Award, 2021 for Excellence in Literature for his book Irrfan: His Life, Philosophy and Shades. He reviews books, many of which have been published in national and international magazines. His last book, The Biography of The Bloodless Battles has been shortlisted for Sahitya Akademi Yuva Puraskar, 2025 and Muse India Young Writer’s Award, 2024. When he is not working, he dreams of the maple.

title: Nurses warn of 'mission impossible' as wards overflow and winter flu crisis hits
summary:
Nurses warn they face a “mission impossible” to keep patients safe as Scottish hospitals overflow during an early, severe winter flu season, with staff describing widespread “corridor care,” shortages of nurses, beds and basic resources, and sustained high demand across A&E and wards. Healthcare bodies report staff are repurposing offices and nooks as treatment spaces, raising concerns for dignity, access to oxygen and patient safety.

Official figures and professional bodies indicate mounting harm and strain: at least 3,629 Significant Adverse Event Reviews (SAERs) were carried out between 2020 and 2024 (a 55% increase over four years), with 1,221 in NHS Greater Glasgow and Clyde and a 120% rise in incidents involving delays to treatment. NHS24 was inundated with calls about flu symptoms, boards have urged people with flu to treat themselves at home where possible, and the Royal College of Emergency Medicine links more than 800 deaths last year to long A&E waits (about a one-third rise on 2023).

Key facts — 3,629 SAERs reported 2020–24 (55% rise); 1,221 in NHS Greater Glasgow and Clyde; delays-to-treatment incidents up 120%. RCN director Colin Pullman highlights normalised corridor care and warns of a hard winter; nurses report being unable to deliver expected care. RCEM reports over 800 deaths associated with long A&E waits (≈33% increase on 2023). Political responses: Scottish Labour’s Jackie Baillie and Scottish Conservative Sandesh Gulhane blame government workforce planning and funding, urging change and frontline resource shifts. Public health advice includes urging eligible people to get flu vaccinations.
importance_reasoning: The report documents substantial excess harm including over 800 deaths linked to long A&E waits and widespread severe flu causing system-wide failures and safety incidents. It therefore meets the stated threshold of involving more than a hundred deaths and describes a large-scale, spreading health crisis placing the health system at risk.
article_text:
Nurses warn of 'mission impossible' as wards overflow and winter flu crisis hits
New figures reveal an explosion in serious incidents involving patients which have had to be reported to regulators as hospitals struggle to cope.
Nurses have said they are facing a “mission impossible” to keep patients safe on overflowing wards as the NHS is plunged into a winter flu crisis. It comes as new figures reveal an explosion in serious incidents involving patients which have had to be reported to regulators as hospitals struggle to cope.
At least 3,629 Significant Adverse Event Reviews (SAER) had to be carried out between 2020 and 2024 - hundreds of which involved unexpected deaths, treatment delays, heart attacks and falls. Scottish Labour has accused the SNP of failing patients after it emerged the number of reviews had soared by 55per cent in just four years.
Scottish Labour health spokesperson Jackie Baillie MSP said: “The NHS has been plunged into crisis because of years of SNP mismanagement. It is deeply worrying that there were more than 3,600 serious patient care incidents in just four years across our health boards.
“This highlights the extent to which services have been pushed to breaking point, with patient safety being put at risk. Frontline teams are being stretched to their limits with no relief in sight, with the SNP having utterly failed to recruit and retain staff.
“This must be the last winter that the SNP are in charge of our health service. Our NHS urgently needs a change in direction.” A staggering 1,221 reviews took place in NHS Greater Glasgow and Clyde alone, while across all health boards issues involving delays to treatment rose 120 percent.
A SAER is carried out and reported to Healthcare Improvement Scotland after an incident that could have caused or did cause harm to a patient, staff member, or visitor. NHS 24 was last week forced to send text messages to thousands of patents warning it had been “inundated” with calls from patients concerned about flu symptoms, especially among school-aged children.
NHS Greater Glasgow and Clyde has asked people with flu to treat themselves at home if at all possible rather than attending A&E. And in an exclusive interview Colin Pullman, director of the Royal College of Nursing Scotland, said he feared an impending crisis, with inappropriate “corridor care” already the norm on wards and a lack of basic resources and staff.
He said: “It is so depressing when you hear the government talking about enhanced plans for winter when we are already in November. We should in fact be talking about winter planning for next year, not this year.
“We are struggling to deal with peaks in demand and there are no real troughs. The issue which has become normalised is corridor care - which is another way of saying care in inappropriate places.
“People are being treated in nooks and crannies in hospitals. We are having to find a room or an office and turn it into a treatment space.
“That is a real issue for people’s dignity and also in relation to access to oxygen and other vital supplies.
“We carried out a survey of staff on corridor care and staff are still saying to us that they are delivering in inappropriate places every day which is putting patients at risk.
“There is not enough nursing staff. We have never got to the point in Scotland where we have employed the number of nurses for which we have vacancies for never mind the actual level that we need.
“Unfortunately we are in for a hard, hard winter. One nurse made a comment in a meeting - she described her work as ‘mission impossible’.
“There is a danger of a real crisis this year as there was last year, especially because of a very bad flu season. I would encourage everyone who is entitled to a flu jab to make sure that they get it.
“Nurses are can do people, day in and day out they do the best that they can but also they get frustrated at not being able to deliver the levels of care that they want to.”
The Royal College of Emergency Medicine (RCEM) has reveal there were more than 800 deaths associated with long A&E waits before admission in Scotland last year - an increase of a third from the 2023 figure.
Pullman added: “It is unforgivable if people are dying because there are not adequate resources in the NHS.
“I look at it in a wider sense as well. Death is a huge thing for nurses and making sure that they get the time to treat families with the dignity and respect they deserve when they have lost a loved one.
“We don’t have enough nurses and there is not enough beds in hospitals because of the amount of people who should be getting care delivered in other places.
“We use that horrible word delayed discharge, but I prefer to say patients who shouldn’t be in hospital because they could be better cared for in the community of the resources were in place.
“When we have rows of ambulances waiting to get into hospitals, that is a symptom that hospitals are full to the brim and that people are probably not getting the treatment they require.”
One nurse at Glasgow’s QEUH who didn’t want to be named said: “It is incredibly busy and there is a lot of flu going about which inevitably ends up hitting staff too.
“The simple fact is that we won’t have the resources in any part of the NHS to cope properly - from A&E to all other department, an it is going to get worse as the winter goes on. It should be a wake up call but unfortunately little ever seems to change.”
Scottish Conservative shadow health secretary Dr Sandesh Gulhane MSP said: “Our NHS is facing a perfect storm as it sleepwalks towards a winter meltdown on the SNP’s watch.
“Years of nationalist underfunding and disastrous workforce planning have pushed staff to the brink.
“Corridor care is now the dangerous norm in our hospitals which is putting lives at risk.
“We’re facing the toughest winter on record, with an early and brutal flu season already putting more pressure on an already overwhelmed system.
“Neil Gray failed to outline his winter NHS plan until the eleventh hour and it wasn’t the worth the paper it was written on.
“He needs to cut the spin and urgently adopt our common-sense plans to cut bureaucracy and get resources to the frontline, otherwise the consequences will be devastating.”
Health Secretary Neil Gray said: “Patient safety is our first priority and our thoughts are with everyone affected by these incidents.
“Learning from significant adverse event reviews must be used to prevent similar events and contribute to continuous improvement across healthcare services.”

title: Ahmed Al-sharaa: Syria's Neutral Stance Masks a Deeper Risk — 3 Strategic Revelations
summary:
Syrian President Ahmed Al-sharaa told an audience at Chatham House in London that Syria will remain neutral in the month‑long conflict pitting the U.S. and Israel against Iran and will not join the fighting unless directly attacked. The confrontation has already spread across the region, causing thousands of casualties and severe disruptions to energy supplies that risk wider market volatility and economic fallout.
Neutrality is presented as a conditional deterrent: it aims to reduce the immediate likelihood of a new front and keeps diplomacy and high escalation thresholds as Damascus’s default response, while the explicit red line — engagement only if attacked — means a single cross‑border incident could rapidly change Syria’s posture. Policymakers and markets therefore face heightened need to monitor cross‑border incidents, diplomatic signaling, and energy supply stability.
Key facts and implications: Chatham House (London) was the venue; speaker Ahmed Al‑sharaa, President of Syria; conflict described as month‑long and involving the U.S., Israel and Iran; reported thousands of casualties and significant energy supply disruptions; neutrality is conditional (engage only if directly attacked) — this narrows immediate escalation risks but creates a clear red line whose breach could prompt rapid Syrian involvement, elevating the risk of regional escalation and global market shocks; operational takeaway — intensify monitoring of border incidents and diplomatic signals.
importance_reasoning: The item describes a month‑long conflict involving the U.S., Israel, and Iran with reported thousands of casualties and major energy disruptions, and notes Syria’s conditional neutrality (a clear red line that could trigger rapid involvement). This involves multiple major powers, large casualty counts, and a significant risk of regional escalation that could spill over into a wider/global conflict — meeting the criteria for existential importance.
article_text:
Ahmed Al-sharaa: Syria’s Neutral Stance Masks a Deeper Risk — 3 Strategic Revelations
Syrian President Ahmed Al-sharaa told an audience at Chatham House in London that Syria intends to remain neutral in the month-long conflict pitting the U. S. and Israel against Iran, and that Damascus will not join the fighting unless directly attacked. His declaration underscores a deliberate posture of restraint even as the confrontation has spread across the region, producing thousands of casualties and severe disruptions in energy supplies that threaten broader economic stability.
Ahmed Al-sharaa and Why This Matters Now
The timing of President Ahmed Al-sharaa’s public statement matters because the conflict’s geographic and economic spillovers are already visible. With the confrontation now a month-long, regional escalation has not been confined to battlefields; disruptions to energy supplies have intensified market uncertainty and raised the prospect of wider economic fallout. By framing neutrality as conditional — binding only until Damascus faces direct aggression — Ahmed Al-sharaa has set a clear red line that both signals restraint and leaves room for rapid change should Syria perceive a threat to its territory or sovereignty.
Deep Analysis: Causes, Implications and Expert Perspective
At the surface, the Syrian stance is straightforward: avoid involvement. Underneath, however, several dynamics explain why neutrality is simultaneously prudent and precarious. The conflict’s spread across the region has produced not only human cost — described as thousands of casualties — but also systemic economic strain through interruptions to energy flows. Those interruptions amplify the risk that localized hostilities could ripple into global markets, reducing energy availability and increasing price volatility.
Strategically, Syria’s neutrality reduces the immediate likelihood of an additional front opening that would further fragment regional dynamics. Diplomacy, as emphasized by President Ahmed Al-sharaa, becomes the default instrument to manage risk and keep escalation thresholds high. His public remarks at Chatham House in London reinforced that diplomatic avenues remain the preferred path for Damascus as long as it is not directly targeted.
Expert perspective is rarely singular in such tense environments, yet the message from the Syrian presidency is unambiguous. Syrian President Ahmed Al-sharaa (President of Syria) stated at the Chatham House event that Syria will avoid entering the conflict unless it faces aggression. That statement frames Damascus’s behavior in deterrence terms: neutrality combined with a declared condition for engagement, designed to signal both non-belligerence and resolve.
Regional and Global Impact — What Comes Next?
The broader consequences of Syria’s declared neutrality are twofold. Regionally, it narrows the list of immediate actors willing to escalate, which can help stabilize certain diplomatic channels. Yet the declaration does not eliminate risk: the conflict’s month-long continuation, the thousands of casualties, and the severe disruptions in energy supplies leave open the possibility of unpredictable shocks. Those shocks could ripple into the global economy, where energy supply interruptions have a multiplier effect on markets and fiscal balances.
Operationally, Syria’s stance places a premium on monitoring cross-border incidents and diplomatic signaling. The country’s vow to remain out of the fighting unless attacked creates a measurable policy hurdle for adversaries contemplating actions that might be construed as aggression against Syrian territory. At the same time, the conditional nature of neutrality means policymakers and markets must remain alert to any incident that might alter Damascus’s calculus.
As the region navigates a conflict now in its month-long phase, the path ahead will depend on whether diplomatic initiatives can outpace military escalations and whether energy supply lines can be stabilized to prevent economic contagion. Syria’s commitment to neutrality, voiced clearly by Ahmed Al-sharaa, offers a potentially stabilizing anchor — but it is an anchor whose hold depends on events that remain volatile and uncertain.
Will declared neutrality hold as the conflict evolves, or will a single cross-border incident be enough to redraw the map of regional involvement?

title: E.U Parliament Halts $174 Million Aid to Tanzania
summary:
The European Parliament has frozen €156 million in development aid for 2026 in response to widespread post-election violence and alleged democratic backsliding following Tanzania’s October 29 general elections. President Samia Suluhu Hassan and the ruling Chama Cha Mapinduzi (CCM) claimed a landslide victory with the president reporting 98% of the vote amid opposition allegations of fraud; the main opposition, Chadema, boycotted after its chairman Tundu Lissu was detained. Election-day protests were met with lethal force, with reports of over 200 protesters killed, thousands arrested, enforced disappearances of activists and journalists, and an internet blackout that curtailed information flow.

European lawmakers condemned the “unacceptable use of violence,” demanded an independent investigation into the killings, called for the release of political prisoners and fresh elections under international oversight, and urged the EU Commission to consider targeted sanctions. The aid freeze targets budget support tied to governance reforms and programs in health and education. International responses from the UN, Amnesty International, the African Union and the Southern African Development Community echoed concerns, citing ballot stuffing, abductions, pre-election crackdowns and calls for non-recognition and transitional justice.

Key facts: €156m in EU development aid for 2026 frozen; Oct. 29 election gave President Hassan a 98% result amid an opposition boycott; Chadema leader Tundu Lissu detained prior to the vote; reports of more than 200 protesters killed and thousands arrested; internet blackout and reports of enforced disappearances of activists and journalists; EU resolution demands independent probe, release of political prisoners, possible targeted sanctions; UN, Amnesty, AU and SADC issued condemnations and urged accountability.
importance_reasoning: Reports of more than 200 people killed, thousands arrested, enforced disappearances and an information blackout meet the stated numeric threshold (>100 deaths) for existential importance. The scale of lethal repression and potential for broader instability, international condemnation and sanctions means this development is globally significant and could contribute to regional destabilization.
article_text:
The European Parliament has frozen €156 million ($174 million) in development aid earmarked for 2026, citing rampant post-election violence and democratic backsliding.
The decision underscores mounting global outrage over the October 29 general elections, which propelled President Samia Suluhu Hassan to a second term amid allegations of fraud and brutality. Hassan’s ruling Chama Cha Mapinduzi (CCM) party secured a sweeping victory, with the president claiming 98% of the vote in a poll opposition leaders branded a “sham.” Chadema, the main opposition, boycotted the vote after its chairman, Tundu Lissu, was detained on spurious charges just weeks prior. What followed was a nationwide protests erupted on election day, met with lethal force from security forces. Reports indicate over 200 protesters killed, thousands arrested, and an internet blackout stifling information flow. Enforced disappearances of activists and journalists have surged, evoking memories of the repressive era under late President John Magufuli.
European lawmakers, in resolution, condemned the “unacceptable use of violence” and demanded an independent probe into the killings, the release of political prisoners, and fresh elections under international oversight. “Tanzania’s authorities have betrayed the democratic aspirations of their people,” declared rapporteur Maria Arena, urging the EU Commission to consider targeted sanctions on perpetrators. The aid freeze targets budget support tied to governance reforms, a lifeline Tanzania relies on for health and education programs.
This EU action amplifies a chorus of international alarm. The United Nations has decried the “hundreds killed and detained” in post-poll clashes, with experts urging an end to disappearances. Amnesty International’s October report, “Unopposed, Unchecked, Unjust,” exposed a “wave of terror” pre-election, including opposition crackdowns and media gags. The African Union lambasted the vote for flouting standards, citing ballot stuffing and abductions that “compromised integrity.” Echoing this, the Southern African Development Community (SADC) condemned the “sham” process in its observer mission’s preliminary statement, joining calls for non-recognition of the results and transitional justice.

title: Grieving Parents in Iran Spend Night at Children Graves, Killed by U.S. Strike
summary:
Families in Minab, Iran gather each night during Ramadan at the cemetery where dozens of children killed in a school strike were buried, laying rugs, lighting candles and praying through the night until dawn. Parents clean headstones, share memories and recite the Quran and prayers beside the small graves; suhoor is observed quietly before dawn as families fold blankets and extinguish candles.
The graves are those of at least 168 children—most girls aged about seven to 12—killed in a Feb. 28 strike on the Shajareh Tayyiba elementary school during the opening hours of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran. Evidence collected by rights groups and media outlets points to a U.S. Tomahawk missile strike; preliminary findings of an internal U.S. military investigation cited outdated targeting data and determined the U.S. was responsible, though the U.S. administration has not acknowledged this.
Key facts: at least 168 child victims (mostly girls, ages ~7–12) killed in single Feb. 28 strike at Shajareh Tayyiba elementary in Minab; evidence and media reporting point to a Tomahawk missile strike and preliminary U.S. military review blamed outdated targeting data while the U.S. has not admitted responsibility; nightly Ramadan vigils include iftar and suhoor rituals, candlelight, Quran recitation and shared memories; several parents quoted by name describe staying overnight beside graves and using communal ritual to cope; published in collaboration with Egab.
importance_reasoning: This describes a single strike that killed at least 168 children—well above the 100-death threshold—and is tied to an active U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, which raises risks of broader escalation. The scale of civilian casualties and the involvement of major powers meet the criteria for existential importance.
article_text:
Grieving Parents in Iran Spend Every Night at the Graves of Their Children, Killed by U.S. Strike
As Ramadan comes to a close, families in Minab, Iran struggle to come to terms with the scale of death, one of the deadliest single attacks on children in memory.
Drop Site is a reader-funded, independent news outlet. Without your support, we can’t operate. Please consider becoming a paid subscriber or making a 501(c)(3) tax-deductible donation today.
MINAB, IRAN—Families arrive at the cemetery after sunset. They come carrying rugs and cushions, food and water, and candles or lanterns that they place on the small, freshly dug graves. Parents carefully clean the tombstones of their buried children. They arrange the spaces around them and settle in for the night—a quiet vigil that will continue until dawn.
The collective grief in Minab, Iran is unfathomable. At least 168 children, most of them girls aged between seven and 12 years old, were killed in a single strike on the Shajareh Tayyiba elementary school on February 28, in the opening hours of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
As the holy month of Ramadan comes to a close this week—a time when prayers carry special weight—families have continued to gather at the cemetery after iftar, the sunset meal to break the fast, to pray beside their dead children in the dark.
Amina Karimi, 42, lost her seven-year-old daughter, Leila, in the strike. She comes to the cemetery every night.
“Ramadan this year arrived carrying a grief I have never known before,” Karimi told Drop Site News. “I read the Quran in a low voice and recite prayers I dedicated to her, and I speak to her as though she can hear me.” She pauses. “Sometimes I close my eyes and recall her laugh, her voice, how she used to run at school, laugh with her friends, and how we used to dream of her future.” Karimi stays at the graveyard through the night despite the cold that cuts through her clothes. “The night is heavy and the cold bites. But the dim candlelight gives me some warmth.”
Evidence collected by human rights groups and media outlets strongly point to the U.S. conducting the Tomahawk missile strike—one of the deadliest single attacks on children in memory. Preliminary findings of an internal U.S. military investigation determined the U.S. was responsible and the school was likely bombed based on outdated targeting data. The Trump administration has not admitted to anything.
In Minab, parents are struggling to come to terms with the scale of the loss.
Reza Zarei’s seven-year-old son, Ali, was killed in the strike. The 45-year-old comes to the cemetery to be beside Ali’s grave through the night until the predawn call to prayer. “I remember the small details of his life,” Zarei told Drop Site. “How he went to school. His friends. His games in the street.” He added, “The night here is silent except for the sounds of prayer and recitation.”
The atmosphere in the cemetery, where all the victims of the school bombing were laid to rest, is unlike anything in the city around it. Sounds disappear. Voices are muted. Families sit or lie down beside the graves, reciting verses, whispering to one another, or falling into long silences. Those who cannot sleep stare at the headstones. The candles planted at the graves create a scattered, uneven light—dozens of small flames that bend in the wind but do not go out. From a distance, the cemetery glows with dim flickering lights.
Reyhana Akbari Far, 40, who lost her eight-year-old daughter Zahra, told Drop Site that she sometimes lies down beside the grave and closes her eyes. “I try to feel her close to me,” she said. “The lit candles around the graves give me some light in the long night, but they cannot remove the pain that fills my heart.” She said the sound of other families nearby—talking, reciting, sharing memories—makes the nights less unbearable. “We exchange memories. We talk about the games our children loved, and we bring back moments of their laughter,” she said. “All of that makes the night a little less lonely, and eases the feeling of absence a little.”
Parents describe the experience of gathering at the cemetery not as mourning in isolation but as a form of continued presence, a refusal to fully accept the distance between the dead and the living. Small children, most of them presumably brothers, sisters, or cousins of the victims, move carefully between the graves. They watch how the adults hold themselves, how grief is organized into ritual, how it is possible to sit with an unbearable thing for hours without breaking apart. They are learning something they are too young to learn.
For Reza Rezaei Pour, 47, the hours in the cemetery are organized around the act of speaking. “I put my hand on the cold stone, I read prayers, and I recall my son Mohammed’s memories,” he told Drop Site about his dead seven-year-old. “His laughs. His play. The small things of his daily life that used to give us happiness.” He describes meeting other fathers in the dark, trading what they remember. “We tell each other about the moments that no longer exist,” he said. “And we learn that shared pain can lighten some of the weight.”
Suhoor—the pre-dawn meal before fasting resumes—passes quietly at the cemetery. Families bring some food but few seem to eat. The ritual of suhoor is observed more than the meal itself. Mothers pour tea from thermoses. A child falls asleep on a father’s shoulder. Someone straightens a candle tilting over in the soft earth.
Fatima Azadi Pezeshki, 43, lost her seven-year-old daughter Huda. She arrives each evening and stays as long as she can. “Sometimes I close my eyes and try to imagine her voice and her image in front of me,” she told Drop Site, “as though she is still present with me.” She said she reads the prayers she used to recite with her daughter at home, in the same order, as a way of keeping something intact. “I try to make her part of these moments despite her physical absence.”
Just before sunrise, families begin to slowly gather their belongings. They fold the blankets, collect the food they barely touched, extinguish the candles. The cemetery empties gradually, family by family, until the dawn breaks and the graves are silent and alone once again.
This story was published in collaboration with Egab.
It’s so frustrating, I’m a combat wounded Vietnam Veteran, after coming home I joined Vietnam Veterans Against The War, the Republicans and Democrats don’t work together unless it’s for war, being an American is shameful.
This is a heart-wrenching piece. For Muslims, this month when revelation came is one of detachment from worldly matters and turning to God. One common belief is that innocent people who are killed -- men, women, children -- are considered martyrs and their next abode is heaven.
As for the perpetrators of this mass murders, most likely our own government, the Muslim belief is that they will be held accountable in this life or the next, but justice will be done either way. Same as in the Biblical phrase of "You reap what you sow."
Let's hope and pray for peace. To paraphrase Dylan, "How may deaths will it take till we know too many people have died?"

title: Cancer drug facility, religious site hit in Israeli-US strikes on Iran
summary:
US and Israeli strikes struck multiple targets across Iran, including Tofigh Daru Research and Engineering Company in Tehran — a major producer of anaesthetics and cancer drugs — and sites in Zanjan, Kermanshah and Isfahan. Authorities report damage to drug-production lines, rescue teams recovering people from rubble after a hit on the Husseiniya Azam congregation hall, at least one killed and several injured, and pro-state demonstrations in Tehran in response to the attacks.

Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) claimed to have struck an Israeli container ship and to have used drones against a group of US marines; the piece cites roughly 1,937 fatalities in Iran since the joint attacks began on Feb. 28 and about 20 killed in Israel. Israeli forces have intercepted many incoming missiles with Iron Dome and David’s Sling, while US and Iranian officials are reported to be in indirect talks — US figures quoted said negotiations are ongoing though mistrust remains.

Outline: Tofigh Daru was identified as state-linked (owned by the Social Security Investment Company) and said to produce active pharmaceutical ingredients across anticancer, narcotics, cardiovascular and immunomodulatory segments, with its production line reportedly damaged; rescue teams extracted two people from Zanjan rubble and one person was killed there; strikes in Kermanshah hit workers at a civilian contracting company (one dead, eight injured); heavy bombing reported around Isfahan, a hub for defence and nuclear sites including Natanz and several airbases; IRGC claimed a ballistic missile hit an Israeli container ship and drones targeted US marines off the UAE coast; quoted officials include Javad Zarif criticizing the strikes, a provincial security official noting strikes hit “military sites,” and US figures saying indirect negotiations continue.
importance_reasoning: The item describes large-scale military strikes with a reported death toll well above 100 (~1,937 fatalities in Iran plus casualties in Israel), cross-border attacks involving US and Israeli forces and Iranian targets, claims of strikes on an Israeli ship and attacks on US marines, and strikes near centres linked to defence and nuclear activity (Isfahan/Natanz). This combination of high fatalities, direct involvement of major powers and their forces, attacks on strategic infrastructure, and clear risk of rapid regional escalation meets the criteria for existential importance (conflict that could escalate into a wider/global conflict). It is not merely an opinion piece or retrospective review.
article_text:
Israeli-US strikes have hit one of Iran’s largest pharmaceutical companies in Tehran, which produces anaesthetics and cancer drugs, the Iranian government said.
Separate attacks were reported on Tuesday at a Shia religious site in the northwestern city of Zanjan, local media said.
- list 1 of 3Three scenarios for the Strait of Hormuz
- list 2 of 3Israel says four soldiers killed as army pushes deeper into south Lebanon
- list 3 of 3Iran war: What is happening on day 32 of US-Israel attacks?
end of list
“During the US and Zionist regime attacks on civilian centres, on the morning of Tuesday, one of the largest companies producing anti-cancer, anaesthetic and specialised medicines was damaged and the drug production line was damaged,” the Iranian government said in a post on X.
The company was identified as Tofigh Daru Research and Engineering Company, which is owned by the Social Security Investment Company, a state-run holding firm. On LinkedIn, Tofigh Daru says it develops and produces active pharmaceutical ingredients “in the anticancer, narcotics, cardiovascular to immunomodulatory segments”.
Iran’s former foreign minister and founder of the Payab Research Institute, Javad Zarif, said on X that Tofigh Daru was targeted by “desperate aggressors” who “failed to realize their diabolical delusions” and “deliberately” struck a medical production facility.
Elsewhere, Iranian Red Crescent teams said rescue operations retrieved two people buried under the rubble after a US-Israeli air strike hit Zanjan’s Husseiniya Azam, a Shia congregation hall next to a mosque.
One person was killed, and eight were injured in Iran’s western Kermanshah province, according to Iran’s Mehr news agency, after US-Israeli strikes targeted workers at a civilian contracting company in the city of Qasr-e Shirin, on the border with Iraq.
Heavy bombing was also reported in Isfahan, which is a key hub for Iran’s defence industry and has major nuclear facilities, including Natanz. The province is home to several major military bases, including the Badr Airbase, the 8th Shekari Airbase and the 4th Air Force Base.
Advertisement
The Fars news agency quoted Akbar Salehi, a security official at the governor’s office in the province, as saying that an initial investigation indicated that strikes targeted some “military sites” without specifying the locations.
The official also said the extent of the damage and any casualties were not immediately clear.
Al Jazeera’s Tohid Asadi, reporting from Tehran, said Iranians have taken to the streets in a pro-state demonstration to express their anger over the continuation of US-Israeli attacks.
Negotiations for a diplomatic resolution of the conflict stalled after US President Donald Trump presented a 15-point plan that Tehran described as “extremely maximalist and unreasonable”.
“There is a cloud of mistrust on Iran’s side,” Asadi said. “Twice over the past 10 months, Iran engaged in a trajectory of diplomacy, when all of a sudden it faced a campaign of air attacks by the US and Israel.”
At least 1,937 have been killed in Iran since the US-Israeli joint attack began on February 28, while 20 people were killed in Israel.
Several impact sites were reported on Tuesday in Tel Aviv, as well as Bnei Brak and Petah Tikva, in central Israel.
Israeli forces have been intercepting most missiles with the Iron Dome and the David’s Sling, designed to shoot down cruise missiles.
Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) claimed in a statement carried by IRNA to have struck an Israeli container ship with a ballistic missile in the Gulf, while Iranian drones also targeted a group of US marines on the UAE coast outside a military base, the statement said.
In an exclusive interview with Al Jazeera on Monday, US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said communication between Tehran and Washington was ongoing, mostly through intermediaries, and that US war objectives would be achieved in “weeks, not months”.
Speaking to reporters on Tuesday, Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth said talks on ending the Iran war are making progress.
“They are very real. They are ongoing, they are active, and I think, gaining strength,” Hegseth said of the negotiations.

title: U.S. Is Burning Through Tomahawk Cruise Missile Stockpile at an Alarming Rate
summary:
Pentagon officials warn that Tomahawk cruise missile stocks are being rapidly depleted during operations against Iran: the U.S. Navy has fired more than 850 Tomahawks, annual production is only a few hundred missiles, and inventory estimates at the start of Operation Epic Fury ranged from roughly 3,000 to 4,500. Each missile can cost up to $3.6 million and take about two years to build; small recent purchases (57 in last year’s budget) and the current burn rate have raised concerns the shortage could affect readiness for future high‑end conflicts such as a war over Taiwan, even as the White House maintains munitions stockpiles are sufficient and efforts to expand production continue.

The conflict’s wider operational and diplomatic picture includes U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iranian nuclear, missile and industrial sites (including the Khondab heavy‑water complex, missile production facilities and major steel plants), Iran’s IRGC declaring the Strait of Hormuz closed and warning shipping, Gulf states taking divergent positions (UAE, Saudi and Bahrain harder‑line; Qatar, Oman and Kuwait favoring a quick end), reports of 10 U.S. service members wounded at Prince Sultan Air Base, deployment movements (USS George H.W. Bush deploying, USS Gerald R. Ford leaving Souda Bay), use of uncrewed surface vessels, F‑35C movements to RAF Lakenheath, CSIS assessments that U.S. strikes are running at roughly 300–500 targets per day while shifting to cheaper stand‑in munitions, consideration of up to 10,000 additional U.S. ground troops, and reporting of extensive Iranian missile/drone activity alongside cyber claims.

Outline of the most salient new facts: more than 850 Tomahawks fired; U.S. Tomahawk stockpile at war start estimated ~3,000–4,500; annual production only a few hundred with only 57 purchased in last year’s budget; per‑round cost up to $3.6M and ~2 years production time; Pentagon officials warn of an ‘‘alarmingly low’’ regional supply and risk of ‘‘Winchester’’ in theater; 10 American service members wounded (two seriously) at Prince Sultan Air Base; reported strikes on Khondab heavy‑water complex, missile production sites (including Yazd) and major steel plants; IRGC declared the Strait of Hormuz closed and vessels have turned back; UAE is willing to join a multinational task force to reopen the Strait and is pursuing a UN mandate with Bahrain; U.S. has deployed USVs and shifted toward stand‑in munitions while striking hundreds of targets daily; consideration of up to 10,000 additional U.S. ground troops; F‑35C carrier‑variant jets have moved to RAF Lakenheath for a land‑based deployment; ThePrint analysis cites ~4,300 missiles and drones launched by Iran since the conflict began, and Reuters reports the Pentagon can so far confirm roughly one‑third of Iran’s missile arsenal destroyed; USS Gerald R. Ford departed Souda Bay.
importance_reasoning: The item describes active and escalating military operations involving the United States (a nuclear-armed power) and Iran: strikes on Iranian nuclear/missile infrastructure, large numbers of missiles/drones launched, declarations closing the Strait of Hormuz, U.S. carrier and force movements, and consideration of major troop deployments. These developments create a significant risk of rapid regional escalation that could draw in other major powers, disrupt global trade and military balances (including munitions readiness for other potential crises), and thereby have global consequences. Therefore it meets the “existential importance” threshold.
article_text:
The Pentagon is alarmed by the rate at which Tomahawk cruise missile stocks have been depleted during ongoing operations against Iran, according to officials who spoke to The Washington Post. The latest development highlights a matter of growing importance for the Department of Defense, which is increasingly looking at the resilience of weapons stocks as it envisages a future high-end scenario in the Pacific, especially a conflict over Taiwan.
So far, the U.S. Navy has fired more than 850 Tomahawk missiles in the war with Iran, officials familiar with the matter told The Washington Post. This has prompted discussions about how more missiles could be made available.
As it stands, only a few hundred examples of the critical long-range strike weapon are manufactured each year, meaning the global supply is limited. The exact number available to the U.S. military at any given time is a closely guarded secret, although the article suggested a higher-end figure of between 4,000 and 4,500 Tomahawk missiles on hand at the start of Epic Fury, and a lower-end figure closer to 3,000 missiles. Again, the Tomahawk would be a primary weapon system used in a conflict with China, where the target sets can range into the tens of thousands, and the country’s anti-access umbrella will require the use of standoff munitions like none other in history.
“The Pentagon has tracked the number of Tomahawks used with an increasing focus on what the burn rate will mean for not only a sustained campaign against Iran but for future military operations as well,” the report states.
One official told The Washington Post that the number of Tomahawks left in the Middle East was “alarmingly low,” while another said that without intervention, the Pentagon is closing in on “Winchester” — military slang meaning out of ammunition — for its supply of the missiles in the region.
The Tomahawk also comes with a hefty price tag: up to $3.6 million for some of the more recent versions, and each round can require up to two years to build. The Navy also faces a problem in that, in recent years, only small batches have been purchased: just 57 examples were included in last year’s defense budget.
At the same time, the Trump administration has repeatedly claimed that critical munitions stockpiles have not been dangerously depleted in the Iran war.
White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said earlier this month that the U.S. military “has more than enough munitions, ammo and weapons stockpiles to achieve the goals of Operation Epic Fury laid out by President Trump — and beyond.”
Every indication we have seen is that for some munitions, that is not reality. The war in Ukraine and constant crisis in the Middle East have depleted those stockpiles, and many of the weapons take years to build, with finite caps on how many can be delivered in any given year. This is a story we have been covering for years. The Trump administration is working to greatly expand production of advanced munitions, but even the fruits of those efforts will take years to realize.
UPDATES:
We have concluded updates for the day.
UPDATE: 10:37 PM EST-
A missile and drone attack on Prince Sultan Air Base in Saudi Arabia has led to significant injuries to U.S. personnel. 10 American service members were wounded, two of which were seriously injured. It isn’t clear what damage was done to aircraft, but we have seen at least one tanker destroyed in recent satellite imagery. This is in addition to the attack that damaged multiple tankers and destroyed another earlier in the war.
Trump is threatening to abandon NATO after member nations did not pitch in with opening the Strait:
He is also joking about renaming the Strait after himself or the U.S.:
Trump is also saying Cuba is next on his list:
As expected, the USS George Washington and its strike group are deploying to the Middle East:
UPDATE: 3:30 PM EST –
Reuters is reporting that Arab states in the Gulf are urging the United States to ensure that any deal with Tehran should do more than end the war. Instead, it must ensure Iranian missile and drone capabilities are “permanently curbed” and that Iran will never again be able to “weaponize” global energy supplies. The agency cites four unnamed Gulf sources.
Meanwhile, it appears that Qatar, Oman, and Kuwait are prioritizing a quick end to the war, while the UAE, Saudi Arabia, and Bahrain are more willing for the fighting to continue, if their longer-term aims are met in any deal to end the war.
UPDATE: 3:10 PM EST –
The Iranian foreign minister, Abbas Araghchi, has confirmed that Israel struck two of its most important steel production facilities, as well as nuclear sites — presumably including the Khondab Heavy Water Complex (see previous updates).
Araghchi said that Friday’s strikes contradicted President Donald Trump’s pledge to postpone attacking Iran’s energy infrastructure for 10 days after he claimed talks were “going well.” He further said that Tehran would exact a “heavy price” for the attacks.
Saudi Arabia wants the U.S. military to ramp up its attacks on Iran, according to a Saudi intelligence source, and the kingdom is meanwhile reportedly also considering joining the fight directly, alongside the United States and Israel. Whether or not Saudi Arabia also starts launching strikes against Iran remains to be seen, but it is the clearest indication so far that the kingdom might become more deeply involved in the conflict, at least at some level.
A Saudi intelligence source confirmed to The Guardian that Riyadh was urging the United States to both continue and intensify the military campaign against Iran. The same source confirmed similar reporting in The New York Times, which states that Saudi Arabia’s de facto leader, Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, had called upon Trump to not curtail Operation Epic Fury, and that the U.S.-Israeli campaign represented a “historic opportunity” to remake the Middle East.
Earlier this week, Trump told journalists that the crown prince is “a warrior. He’s fighting with us.”
At this point, however, there are no reports of active Saudi military involvement in the conflict, but the kingdom is now at least weighing up that option, if peace efforts fail.
This week, The Wall Street Journal and Jerusalem Post both reported that Saudi Arabia has decided to open up additional military bases for the use of the U.S. military in its operations against Iran. Reportedly, the facilities include King Fahd Air Base in Taif in western Saudi Arabia.
Saudi Arabia has come under direct Iranian attack since the start of Epic Fury, including a drone strike last week on the oil refinery in Yanbu on the Saudi Red Sea coast.
At the same time, Saudi oil exports are not as vulnerable as those of other countries in the region, so it has not suffered to the same degree as other Gulf states. Much of Saudi Arabia’s oil exports are carried by a pipeline to the Red Sea, purposefully avoiding the Strait of Hormuz.
There is also the threat that the Iran-backed Houthis in Yemen, which have long waged a separate war with Saudi Arabia, could be drawn into the current conflict if the Saudi position changes.
Were that to happen, the vital Red Sea oil pipeline could become a very prominent target for Iran and the Houthis.
Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) declared today that shipping “to and from ports of allies and supporters of the Israeli-American enemies” is prohibited through any corridor or to any destination, Iranian state media reported.
The IRGC added that the Strait of Hormuz remains closed and any attempted transit through the strategic waterway will face “harsh measures.”
